title: Using at least basic drinking water sources in urban vs. rural areas
subtitle: Share of urban versus rural population using at least a basic drinking water source; that is, an [improved source](#dod:improved-water-source) within 30 minutes round trip to collect water.
caption:
Using at least basic drinking water sources in urban vs. rural areas

Share of urban versus rural population using at least a basic drinking water source; that is, an improved source within 30 minutes round trip to collect water.

This scatter plot compares the share of urban versus rural populations using at least basic drinking water sources in 2024. The horizontal axis represents the share of the rural population, ranging from 0% to 100%, while the vertical axis represents the share of the urban population, also ranging from 0% to 100%. A diagonal line indicates where urban and rural shares would be equal. Most data points sit above this line, showing higher urban coverage than rural coverage across the dataset. Points are color-coded by region: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. Countries in Europe and North America cluster tightly in the top-right corner, with both urban and rural shares near or at 100%. In contrast, several African nations appear in the lower-left quadrant. The Central African Republic sits near 30% for rural and just under 50% for urban. DR Congo is positioned around 15% rural and 60% urban. Eritrea, South Sudan, Chad, and Angola fall between 30% and 50% on the rural axis, with urban shares ranging from 65% to 90%. Gabon, Congo, and Cameroon sit in the mid-range, with rural shares between 50% and 60% and urban shares above 80%. Burundi, Togo, Benin, and Liberia show rural shares between 60% and 75%, with urban shares mostly above 80%. Afghanistan, Albania, Brazil, Comoros, and Sao Tome and Principe are grouped near the top-right, with rural shares above 80% and urban shares at or near 100%. The visual distribution shows a clear gradient: as rural access increases, urban access tends to be higher, with the largest gaps occurring in countries with low rural coverage.
